this activity diagram describes the process of viewing emergency situations within a system.
the process starts with an initial authentication check.
if the user is authenticated, they can click "visualizza emergenze" to check for available emergencies.
if no emergencies are available, a message indicating "nessuna emergenza" is displayed.
if the user is not authenticated, they need to log in first before being able to view emergencies.
the diagram outlines the main flow and two potential error paths (user not authenticated or no emergencies available).

utente registrato? -> no — type=control_flow; label="no"; explanation="user not registered"
utente registrato? -> si — type=control_flow; label="si"; explanation="user is registered"
no -> registrazione utente — type=control_flow; label="no"; explanation="redirect to user registration"
si -> login — type=control_flow; label="si"; explanation="proceed to login"
login -> inserisci dati login — type=control_flow; label="inserisci dati login"; explanation="prompt for login data"
inserisci dati login -> dati corretti? — type=control_flow; label="dati corretti?"; explanation="validate login data"
dati corretti? -> no — type=control_flow; label="no"; explanation="login data incorrect"
dati corretti? -> si — type=control_flow; label="si"; explanation="login data correct"
si -> home — type=control_flow; label="si"; explanation="access home page"
flow 1: start -> utente registrato? -> si -> login -> inserisci dati login -> dati corretti? -> si -> home
flow 2: start -> utente registrato? -> no -> registrazione utente
this activity diagram models the process of user login and registration.
the main flow starts at the "start" node, checks if the user is registered, and proceeds accordingly.
if the user is not registered, it prompts for registration.
if the user is registered, it prompts for login credentials and validates them.
if the login credentials are correct, the user accesses the home page.
there is a secondary flow for users who are not registered, guiding them through the registration process.
the diagram includes decision points to handle both successful and unsuccessful login attempts.
click "inserisci emergenza" -> coordinatore autenticato? — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="flow to authentication check"
coordinatore autenticato? -> no — type=control-flow; label="no"; explanation="branch for unauthenticated coordinator"
coordinatore autenticato? -> sì — type=control-flow; label="sì"; explanation="branch for authenticated coordinator"
coordinatore autenticato? -> inserisci dettagli — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="flow to detail input"
inserisci dettagli -> informazioni complete? — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="flow to information completeness check"
informazioni complete? -> no — type=control-flow; label="no"; explanation="branch for incomplete information"
informazioni complete? -> sì — type=control-flow; label="sì"; explanation="branch for complete information"
informazioni complete? -> aggiorna dettagli — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="flow to update details"
aggiorna dettagli -> informazioni complete? — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="loop back to information completeness check"
aggiorna dettagli -> coordinatore autenticato? — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="loop back to authentication check"
coordinatore autenticato? -> errore — type=control-flow; label="no"; explanation="branch for error handling"
errore -> - none — type=control-flow; label="->"; explanation="flow to end"
flow 1: click "inserisci emergenza" -> coordinatore autenticato? -> sì -> inserisci dettagli -> informazioni complete? -> sì -> crea emergenza
flow 2: click "inserisci emergenza" -> coordinatore autenticato? -> no -> errore
this activity diagram models the process of creating an emergency report.
the process starts with clicking "inserisci emergenza".
it checks if the coordinator is authenticated. if not, it handles an error.
if authenticated, it proceeds to input details.
it then checks if the provided information is complete. if not, it updates the details and loops back to the information completeness check.
if the information is complete, it creates the emergency report.
there are two distinct error paths: one for an unauthenticated coordinator and one for incomplete information.

coordinatore autenticato? -> errore — type=control-flow; label="no"; explanation="authentication failed"
coordinatore autenticato? -> seleziona emergenza — type=control-flow; label="sì"; explanation="coordinator authenticated"
seleziona emergenza -> seleziona compito — type=control-flow; label="—"; explanation="emergency selected"
seleziona compito -> volontario assegnato? — type=control-flow; label="—"; explanation="task selected"
volontario assegnato? -> errore — type=control-flow; label="no"; explanation="volunteer not assigned"
volontario assegnato? -> rimuovi volontario — type=control-flow; label="sì"; explanation="volunteer assigned"
flow 1: click "visualizza emergenza" -> coordinatore autenticato? -> seleziona emergenza -> seleziona compito -> volontario assegnato? -> rimuovi volontario
this activity diagram models the process of removing a volunteer from an emergency task.
the process starts with clicking "visualizza emergenza" to view an emergency.
if the coordinator is not authenticated, an error occurs.
if authenticated, the user selects an emergency and then a task.
if a volunteer is not assigned to the task, an error occurs.
if a volunteer is assigned, the user can remove the volunteer from the task.
key edge cases include authentication failure and volunteer assignment status checks.

server -> database — type=message; label="select * from utenti where email = ?"; explanation="server queries database for user"
server -> server — type=message; label="user"; explanation="server retrieves user data"
server -> server — type=message; label="user.length == 0"; explanation="server checks user length"
server -> server — type=message; label="bcrypt.comparesync(password, user.password)"; explanation="server compares passwords"
server -> server — type=message; label="genera jti e token jwt"; explanation="server generates jwt token"
server -> database — type=message; label="update tokens set revoked = true where id = ?"; explanation="server updates tokens"
server -> server — type=message; label="risultato update"; explanation="server receives update result"
server -> database — type=message; label="insert into tokens (jti, id)"; explanation="server inserts new token"
server -> server — type=message; label="risultato insert"; explanation="server receives insert result"
server -> client — type=message; label="risposta (token o errore)"; explanation="server sends response back to client"

flow 1: client -> server (post /login) -> server -> database (select * from utenti where email = ?) -> server -> server (user) -> server -> server (user.length == 0) -> server -> server (bcrypt.comparesync(password, user.password)) -> server -> server (genera jti e token jwt) -> server -> database (update tokens set revoked = true where id = ?) -> server -> server (risultato update) -> server -> database (insert into tokens (jti, id)) -> server -> server (risultato insert) -> server -> client (risposta (token o errore))
the login process starts with the client sending a post request to the server.
the server queries the database for a user matching the provided email.
if the user exists, the server compares the password using bcrypt.
if authentication is successful, the server generates a jwt token and updates the tokens table.
finally, the server sends a response back to the client containing either a token or an error message.
the process involves multiple database interactions and server-side logic to ensure secure authentication.

flow 2: client -> server (post /nuovocompito) -> server -> verifytoken() -> server -> logger (logger.error(error)) -> server -> client (errore http (400/401/500))
this diagram models a sequence of interactions between a client, server, database, and logger for creating a new task.
the main flow involves the client sending a post request to the server, which then verifies a token, inserts data into the database, and returns a result to the client.
an alternative path exists where an error is logged and an http error is returned to the client.
key edge cases include successful task creation and handling errors such as unauthorized access or server issues.
client -> server — type=message; label="post /terminaemergenza"; explanation="client sends post request"
server -> database — type=message; label="esegui update"; explanation="server updates database"
database -> server — type=message; label="result (numero emergenze terminate)"; explanation="database returns result"
flow 1: client -> server -> verifytoken() -> server -> database -> server -> client
this diagram models a sequence of interactions between a client, server, and database to handle the termination of emergencies.
the main flow starts with the client sending a post request to the server.
the server verifies the token and updates the database.
depending on the result, the server sends a message back to the client indicating either successful termination of emergencies or no emergencies terminated.
there is an error path indicated for invalid tokens, which terminates the process.
